petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Chinese language lecturer seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in education under EB-1A. She serves as the director of the Chinese program at a university (name redacted). She authored scholarly articles in professional publications and claims contributions to Chinese language instruction and curriculum development.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed a 'National Grant' and a 'Distinguished Scholar Grant.' For the National Grant, she initially provided a letter stating she was 'awarded a scholarship under the State Scholarship Fund,' then screenshots from edu.cn regarding the 'National Scholarship Fund,' and on appeal a letter referencing the 'National Grant.' For the Distinguished Scholar Grant, she submitted a document reflecting receipt of the 'Top National Research Grant' for the 'Young Scholar Research Grant,' a letter discussing the 'National Funding Project,' and on appeal letters discussing the 'Distinguished Scholar Grant' and 'National Social Science Fund.' She also cited 'Outstanding Teaching' awards, a bilingual speech contest award, and 'numerous funding awards.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence referenced inconsistent names (National Grant vs. National Scholarship Fund; Distinguished Scholar Grant vs. National Funding Project vs. National Social Science Fund), making it unclear which grant she actually received, citing Matter of Ho, 19 I&N Dec. 582 (BIA 1988). Additionally, the initial document and a support letter referenced different projects as the basis for the Distinguished Scholar Grant, and one letter appeared to have lines in a different font suggesting possible alteration. Substantively, the AAO held that scholarships, grants, and fellowships—even if recognized in academia—are not tantamount to nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards for excellence. The AAO noted academic study is training for a field, not a field of endeavor, and financial support for training or research does not qualify as a prize or award for excellence. The petitioner also did not argue that her other teaching/contest awards were nationally or internationally recognized.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an article about her published in China Daily USA and posted on usa.chinadaily.com.cn, and four television still shots from WDAZ 8-TV (ABC affiliate) from 2016 with Wikipedia screenshots indicating WDAZ 'reaches only 82,000 American television households' and 'an additional 256,000 homes [in Manitoba].' She also provided circulation and viewing statistics for China Daily and chinadaily.com.cn.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner claimed coverage in China Daily and chinadaily.com.cn but the actual article was in China Daily USA and usa.chinadaily.com.cn, and her circulation/viewing statistics pertained to the parent publication rather than the US edition, so she did not show the US edition was a major medium. For WDAZ, she did not submit a transcript of the broadcast establishing published material about her, did not include the full date and author, and did not demonstrate WDAZ is a major medium. The AAO discounted the Wikipedia evidence (citing Badasa v. Mukasey) noting Wikipedia cannot guarantee content validity, and noted the figures described households 'reached' rather than actual viewers of the specific news broadcast.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of downloads (17 downloads of her syllabus between January 22, 2018 and September 4, 2018, and 1,700+ downloads of her book), citations (her highest cited article received 21 citations with remaining articles garnering 8 or less, total of 45 citations since 2000), comparative data from Clarivate Analytics indicating the average author in general social sciences receives 4.95 citations over a ten-year period (2003-2013), and five recommendation letters. Letters described her introducing online programs, expanding traditional textbooks with digital tools, implementing original strategies and a new Chinese curriculum online at two universities, and discussed her upcoming conference presentation on digitization of Chinese course development.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the 17 syllabus downloads occurred after the petition was filed, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1) requiring eligibility at time of filing. Downloads alone do not demonstrate implementation or wide application in the field. The 1,700 book downloads similarly were not shown to be significant or tantamount to major contributions. For the citations, one article with 1,247 downloads only garnered 21 citations, which did not reflect major significance. The AAO noted the petitioner did not show that exceeding the 4.95 average citation rate reflects contributions of major significance. The recommendation letters described her contributions at two universities but did not demonstrate how her curriculum/teaching strategies significantly affected the field as a whole. The letters also speculated on potential future influence ('upcoming,' 'will be followed,' 'beginning to be incorporated,' 'future scholars will build on this'), which does not establish already-realized major significance. The AAO cited Kazarian (580 F.3d 1030) noting publications/presentations alone are insufficient without evidence of major significance, and noted letters with conclusory hyperbolic language are not probative, citing 1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner authored scholarly articles in professional publications.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined and the AAO agreed that the petitioner fulfilled the scholarly articles criterion under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed a leading role as Director of the Chinese Language program at a university from 2015-2016. She submitted two letters from a former dean of a college and two letters from the university president confirming her role, and referenced the China Daily USA article and an article from the Grand Forks Herald. The articles discuss enrollment increase in the Chinese program from 5 to 50 students.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters and articles did not establish the petitioner held a leading position at the university as a whole. Her role as director of the Chinese language program was a far more subordinate role compared to a dean or president. While enrollment in the Chinese program increased from 5 to 50, she did not show this was leading to the university overall rather than limited to a program within the university. Even if her role were considered critical, the evidence only discussed her impact on the Chinese program rather than on the success or standing of the university in general. The petitioner did not argue critical role in her brief, only leading role.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed because the petitioner satisfied only one of the ten regulatory criteria (scholarly articles) when at least three are required. The AAO therefore did not need to conduct a final merits determination under Kazarian, but nevertheless reviewed the record in aggregate and concluded it did not support the required sustained national or international acclaim. The AAO emphasized EB-1A is a highly restrictive classification for individuals already at the top of their fields, not those progressing toward the top, and the petitioner did not demonstrate her work reflects a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
1756, Inc. v. The U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
Matter of Skirball Cultural Ctr., 25 I&N Dec. 799 (AAO 2012)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO flagged potential evidence tampering: the last two lines of one support letter regarding the Distinguished Scholar Grant appeared to be in a different font than the rest of the letter, 'suggesting it may have been altered or not authored by him.' The AAO noted that unresolved material inconsistencies may lead it to reevaluate the reliability and sufficiency of other evidence, citing Matter of Ho. Additionally, the petitioner submitted inconsistent information about the names of her claimed grants/awards and the projects for which they were received, which the AAO treated as unresolved inconsistencies.
